title: NAC Policy Compliance Trending
spl:
index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:auth" earliest=-30d
| eval outcome=case(match(lower(authorization_result),"(?i)permit|access.accept"),"permit", match(lower(authorization_result),"(?i)deny|reject"),"deny", true(),"other")
| timechart span=1d count by outcome
description: Daily percentage of authentications that receive “permit” vs “deny” vs “redirect” per authorization policy — trending for policy drift and rollout validation.
value: Network security teams measure whether the actual NAC authorization profiles assigned to devices match the intended policy design, detecting policy shadowing, default-rule overuse, and devices receiving more or less network access than intended.
implementation: Normalize `authorization_result` from your ISE field set. Alert when `deny` share increases >2× baseline week-over-week.
visualization: Stacked area (outcomes over time), Line chart (deny rate), Single value (deny % today).
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  by All_Traffic.user, All_Traffic.action, All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest span=1h
| sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Authentication pattern spikes during ISE or RADIUS maintenance, certificate renewal windows, or RADIUS time-outs that affect many clients at once.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Cisco ISE with multiple authorization policies. Data in `index=nac` with `sourcetype=cisco:ise:syslog`. Key fields: `AuthorizationPolicyMatchedRule`, `SelectedAuthorizationProfiles`, `EndPointPolicy`, `CallingStationID`, `UserName`, `IdentityGroup`.
• NAC policy compliance means: every device that authenticates receives the authorization profile (and therefore network access level) that your security policy intends. Deviations include: devices hitting the default rule (no specific policy matched), devices assigned an incorrect profile, or devices bypassing intended controls.
• Create a `nac_intended_policy.csv` lookup: `identity_group,endpoint_profile,expected_authz_rule,expected_authz_profile` — the design intent for each identity/device combination.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Verify policy matching data:
```spl
index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:syslog" earliest=-24h
| where match(AuthenticationStatus, "(?i)pass")
| stats count dc(CallingStationID) as devices by AuthorizationPolicyMatchedRule, SelectedAuthorizationProfiles
| sort - count
```

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

Primary search — Policy compliance check:
```spl
index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:syslog" earliest=-24h
| where match(AuthenticationStatus, "(?i)pass")
| dedup CallingStationID sortby -_time
| lookup nac_intended_policy.csv identity_group as IdentityGroup endpoint_profile as EndPointPolicy OUTPUT expected_authz_rule expected_authz_profile
| eval policy_compliant=if(SelectedAuthorizationProfiles=expected_authz_profile OR isnull(expected_authz_profile), "Compliant", "Non-Compliant")
| stats count by policy_compliant
| eventstats sum(count) as total
| eval pct=round(count/total*100,1)
```

Understanding this SPL: Compares the actual authorization profile each device received against the intended profile from the policy design document. Non-compliant means the device got a different profile than expected — either more access (security risk) or less access (operational issue). A common cause: ISE evaluates policies top-down and the first matching rule wins, so a broadly matching rule placed too high can shadow more specific rules below it.

Default-rule hits (no specific policy matched):
```spl
index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:syslog" earliest=-24h
| where match(AuthenticationStatus, "(?i)pass") AND match(AuthorizationPolicyMatchedRule, "(?i)default")
| stats count by IdentityGroup, EndPointPolicy, SelectedAuthorizationProfiles
| sort - count
```

Step 3 — Validate
(a) Compare ISE Policy Sets page with the lookup to ensure alignment.
(b) Connect a device that should match a specific rule and verify the correct rule matches.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard: Row 1 — Compliance rate trend. Row 2 — Non-compliant breakdown. Row 3 — Default rule hits.
Report: Weekly compliance report to network security management.

Runbook:
1. **Non-compliant devices with too much access** — Immediate security review of the ISE policy set.
2. **Default rule hits increasing** — New device types or identity groups not covered by policy.
3. **After ISE policy change** — Run compliance check immediately to detect unintended shadowing.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **All devices show compliant** — The lookup may be too permissive. Ensure it has entries for all identity/device combinations.
• **`AuthorizationPolicyMatchedRule` empty** — Enable detailed RADIUS logging in ISE.
grandmaExplanation: We have a plan for exactly what network access each type of device should get. We check whether the system is actually following that plan, because sometimes rules overlap and devices end up with more access than they should have — like giving a visitor the keys to the whole building.
